figures.caption: Table 1: Comparison with the state-of-the-art temporal action detection approaches. We report AP at different tIoU.
Fig. 5: Visualization of our phase predictions against the ground-truth data on 4D-OR test set.

Fig. 3: Pipelines of our methods for the downstream tasks (FC = Fully-Connected, TAD = Temporal Activity Detection). (1) Role prediction: we use GazeLLE [31] and tracking to generate heatmap tracklets, and pass them through a transformer encoder to classify the roles. (2) Phase recognition: we fuse features of gaze heatmaps and head positions of each frame, pass sequential frame-level features through a transformer encoder along with learnable role embeddings to obtain clip-level features, and pass all the clip-level features through a temporal convolution network to classify the surgical phases. (3) Team communication detection: we train a spatial-temporal transformer that encodes gaze features for each clip through contrastive learning with visual features [38], merge gaze, visual and action features [9] through gated fusion, and then detect the activities through a TAD model [6].

Fig. 4: Analysis of deviation between head orientation and gaze signal using the EgoExOR dataset [25] with 76,603 frames across multiple surgical procedures. (Left) Distribution of angular deviation between the head-worn camera’s optical axis and gaze. (Right) 2D spatial distribution of gaze relative to the head center (frame center at (0,0)), where image resolution is 1408*1408.
